optionA: Throws an exception if the key already exists
optionB: Inserts a new value only if the key is absent or maps to null
optionD: Returns null if the key is absent
explanation: computeIfAbsent() checks if the key is missing (or null), then calls the mapping function and stores its result. It's the standard idiom for lazy-initializing values in a Map.

questionText: In Java, what is the output of: int x = 5; System.out.println(x++ + " " + ++x);
explanation: x++ returns 5 (post-increment, x becomes 6), then ++x pre-increments x to 7 and returns 7. Output: '5 7'.

questionText: What is the average-case time complexity of HashMap.get(key) in Java?
explanation: HashMap uses hashing — the key is hashed to a bucket index, making lookup O(1) on average. Worst case is O(n) when all keys collide, but this is rare with a good hash function.

questionText: Which algorithm is BEST suited for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
explanation: BFS explores nodes level by level, guaranteeing it finds the shortest path (fewest edges) in an unweighted graph. Dijkstra is needed for weighted graphs; DFS doesn't guarantee shortest path.

questionText: What is the default scope of a Spring Bean?
optionA: prototype — a new instance per injection
optionB: request — a new instance per HTTP request
optionC: singleton — one shared instance per ApplicationContext
optionD: session — one instance per HTTP session
explanation: Spring beans are singleton by default — one instance is created and shared across the entire ApplicationContext. Use @Scope("prototype") to get a new instance per injection point.

questionText: What does @Transactional(readOnly = true) do in Spring?
optionA: Prevents all database writes and throws an exception if any write is attempted
optionB: Hints to the JPA provider and DB to optimise for read-only workloads (no dirty checking)
optionC: Opens a database connection but does not start a transaction
explanation: readOnly=true is a performance hint — Hibernate skips dirty checking on loaded entities (they can't be modified), and some DB drivers route the query to a read replica. It does NOT enforce immutability at the Java level.

questionText: What is the key difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN?
optionA: INNER JOIN returns all rows from both tables; LEFT JOIN returns only matching rows
optionB: LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table (including non-matching), INNER JOIN returns only matching rows
optionC: They produce identical results but LEFT JOIN is faster
optionD: INNER JOIN works on indexed columns only; LEFT JOIN works on all columns
explanation: INNER JOIN returns rows only when both sides match. LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table — non-matching right-side columns are NULL. This is the standard way to include rows with no related records.

questionText: Adding an index on a large table typically makes INSERT statements:
optionA: Faster, because indexed inserts skip duplicate checks
optionB: The same speed, because indexes don't affect writes
optionC: Slower, because each insert must also update the index data structure
optionD: Faster only on primary key columns, slower on all others
explanation: Every INSERT (and UPDATE/DELETE) must maintain all indexes on the table. More indexes = more work per write. This is the classic read-vs-write trade-off — indexes speed up SELECTs but slow down writes.

questionText: What is the key difference between Amazon SQS and Amazon SNS?
optionA: SQS is for email delivery; SNS is for queuing jobs
optionB: SQS is a pull-based message queue (one consumer); SNS is push-based pub/sub (many subscribers)
optionC: SNS stores messages durably; SQS does not
optionD: They are identical services with different pricing models
explanation: SQS is a queue — messages sit until a consumer polls and processes them (point-to-point). SNS is pub/sub — a single message is pushed to all subscribers simultaneously (fan-out). They're often combined: SNS fans out to multiple SQS queues.

questionText: Which AWS service provides a managed Redis or Memcached cache?
explanation: Amazon ElastiCache is the fully-managed in-memory cache service supporting both Redis and Memcached. DAX is specifically DynamoDB's cache; RDS is relational DB; CloudFront is a CDN.
